speaker: interviewer
text: You were speaking of a final message and relating back to something you had said about when you were in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, I just wanted to say that when-- you know, we had our ups and downs in Bergen-Belsen. Someday we hoped that, after all, we will get to Palestine, as we were originally promised. Other days, we were afraid that we will wind up in Auschwitz or thereabouts. And, you know, frequently when-- it was hard to sleep at night. And so I have these, these visions of how I'm going to get a knife, if I survive, and run through the streets of Budapest, and kill all of these people who were responsible for this situation in which we are. And I had literally, you know, visions of individuals that I would kill, probably student organization, neighbors, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people who, who, who did us in. And so I was engaging in these dreams of revenge. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, you know, things kind of changed. I, I guess I aged in the camp and became a bit more mature. And it became clear to me that that's not the solution but that really, what I ought to do is to do whatever I can that the kind of discrimination against people that was practiced on us should not take place anymore. And so I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just-- I made a vow in Bergen-Belsen, to which I kept the rest of my life, that I will not break bread with anybody who discriminates against other humans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm somewhat proud that I kept to it. I walked out of board of directors meetings where, where discriminatory language was used. I broke with people, not to talk with them anymore. I refused to have lunch with a group of people. And, and I feel strongly about that. I feel that, that respect for other human beings. And unwillingness to discriminate against others on account of anything is really a mandatory thing for good life of people on this earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. We're going to meet your wife. And then you'll show us your photos. Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to just ask you what your ultimate feelings were about Dr. Kasztner, particularly in light of his being murdered by Jews in Israel a number of years after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a difficult question, a difficult question to answer. From what I know-- and this is very subjective-- from what I know, Rezso Kasztner was a hero. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not just in the sense that he saved my family's and my lives-- some of us might have survived anyway. But so many of us survived from the family due to him that, you know, he can be only a hero. And also my personal relationship with him, it was like a relationship to an uncle. And it was all goodwill on his part. You know, the way he behaved to me during those Sunday lunches, which to me are, are, you know, important memories of my life, even though I was just a by-sitter and most of the conversation took place between him and my father. But that-- the fact that he went out of his way to procure a, a visa for-- a certificate for Palestine for me, which then was scotched by my [LAUGHS] father, who wouldn't let me go. His behavior to my girlfriend-- I mean, all that was, you know, positive. Yeah, there is a moral question. I have no question about the fact that he didn't enrich himself. It is known that he, he died in, in very modest financial circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he, he didn't benefit financially.

speaker: interviewer
text: But yes, there is a moral question. What about designating people for life, and other people-- not participating in that? Now, it's not true that he designated people for death. But he designated people for life. And there is an implication there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, people who have lost their beloved ones, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they'll become very emotional about the, the judge who sits in judgment over life and death, even though he does not decree death. But he decrees life to some, which he doesn't decree for others. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a difficult question to answer. But on the whole, I feel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel he did the right thing, under the circumstances. He had very difficult choices to make.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only people with whom he could deal at that time were the Germans. Nobody else had the power of giving life. And life for some is better than life for none. And that is what he fundamentally did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, with all sympathies and respect for the people who feel that they were disadvantaged in that process-- you know, I asked myself many times, what would I have done? Probably would have done the same thing-- would have done what he did. OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now would you please introduce us to the lovely woman on your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. This is my wife, Edith. And, you know, after the Holocaust, when we met in 1948, which was now almost 50 years ago-- it was 48 years ago-- the best thing I ever did was to run into her. And, you know, we talked about Holocaust experiences. But, as I told you, those three months in Budapest, three months and a few days, were more important than any other period, no matter what, how long. And the, the five months or so in Bergen-Belsen were really unimportant. It's-- you know, I never think of that. That's unimportant. I had some interesting experiences watching Hanover being bombed and rejoicing, you know. I don't want to diverge. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you do rejoice when your enemies suffer, particularly when you are at the end of your-- of your ability to suffer. You do rejoice when, when Hanover was bombed. We saw Hanover burning from the camp. And that was the time when we could have escaped because all our guards went underground. They, you know, they sought refuge wherever they went. Of course, there would have no point of escaping. They would have caught us within hours afterward. But, you know, a month, all that was over and sort of-- not forgotten, but buried. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life came back. And a lot of other things came back. Good life came back. And interest in profession came. And success in profession came and the number of these very everyday things that are nowhere as dramatic as the-- as the Holocaust. But that was what life was really about. And we-- on the whole, we had a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have occasional problems, but, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you'd like to add? I know you've already told your story. But sitting here with your husband, is there anything that you would like to add for us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We, for many years talked very little about, about the Holocaust and about the years. There was a tendency to live life now and to go on. And I think a lot of other survivors have done that. And we had children. And children grew up. And we-- you know, they-- we enjoyed them. And they, they had grandparents they, they felt very positive about. And I, I feel, in general, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had very positive experiences. And the kids make this life even more worthwhile-- seeing the children, the grandchildren. And-- but if at any time, our grandchildren will see those tapes, it's important for us. Because it is important that this-- such a document exists. And I'm grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we thank you both very much. And now, we'll look at your photographs, including of your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. These are my parents on their wedding day in Budapest, Hungary, on March 21, 1923-- my father, Leo Stern, and my mother, Bertha [? Leah ?] Frankfurter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my parents' family, taken sometime around 1931, I don't know where. My father, Leo Stern, standing to the right; my mother, Bertha Stern, to the left; and below them, myself, roughly age six; and on the other side, my brother, roughly age three. And between them, probably a governess, whose name I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and my name is Arthur. And my brother's name is Robert.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding picture of our extended family, the family Stern, that was taken on December 17, 1933. The occasion is the wedding of my uncle Jonas to my cousin Anne. Jonas married his sister's daughter, Anne. You see the entire family on the picture. Standing in the background are-- in the middle, my aunt Yeti, the only daughter of my grandparents on my father's side. Also standing are, from left to right, my uncle Joseph, my uncle Herman, my father Leo, then again, Yeti in the middle, my uncle Solomon, who perished in Auschwitz, and my uncle [? Edmund. ?] Sitting among the adults sitting are my aunt Lily or [? Susie, ?] as she was called, the wife of Joseph, who stands on the left-hand side; my aunt Lily, who is-- was the wife of my uncle Herman; my mother, standing just-- sitting just below my father; then my grandmother, on my father's side, my father's mother; the bride, Anne; the groom, Jonas. On the right side of Jonas, my grandfather, Elias-- Moses Elias; and my aunt [? Bella, ?] who sits just below her husband [? Edmund. ?] And then you have the kids, who stand respectively close to their parents. You have myself and my brother Robert, close to our parents, sort of the left-hand, middle side. On the right extreme side is my cousin [? Stefanie, ?] the daughter of [? Edmund ?] and [? Bella. ?] Sitting on the floor are, are other cousins whose names I don't remember. I can't quite-- can't quite visualize them. Sitting on the floor on the right hand side is Alfred, who is one of the-- who is the son of [? Edmund ?] and [? Bella. ?] In the middle is-- I think that was Judith. She died before the war from cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, sit back.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left-hand side sitting-- oh, it must be the son [? Laurent ?] of Herman and Lily. And on the extreme left side standing, is, is my cousin Gabi, daughter of Herman and Lily.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my maternal grandparents, the Frankfurters-- Michael, my grandfather, and Fanny, my grandmother. The picture was taken about 1935, about a year or two before my grandfather passed away. My grandmother was killed in Budapest on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, I think, in their summer home. Or they're close by, in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document, a protective pass issued by the German and Hungarian authorities shortly after the occupation of Hungary to people who, at least temporarily, have protection against deportation or imprisonment or what have you. It was issued to my father. And his picture is in front. If I recall correctly, it has no date. It's-- was issued, no doubt, in April 1944. And it lists the family members who are also protected under this document. We went around with copies of this. And if stopped, to identify ourselves, we showed it. And this prevented our arrest or deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Leo Stern.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken on our wedding day. And it shows Edith, my wife-- born Edith Samuel-- and myself, Arthur Stern, in the Grand Hotel Montreux Territet, which is in Switzerland, on September 17, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken sometime in March, 1951, on the ship My dad-- my mom, who isn't on the picture-- but my dad, who is on the left-hand side, Leo Stern; and I am on the right hand side, Arthur Stern, on our way to America when we immigrated there. We actually immigrated on March 19, 1951, the day of the Hungarian-- the beginning of the Hungarian Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Arthur Stern, made in late 1953 or early 1954, showing the first transistor radio, created and developed by me, by Arthur Stern, while working for the General Electric Company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Syracuse, New York, in the General Electric Laboratory, and was published all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our family-- that is, Edith's and my family-- taken in September, 1995, in Westfield, New Jersey, I believe the day after Rosh Hashanah or before Rosh Hashanah, I'm not sure. It shows standing in the rear, myself, Arthur Stern; Edith, next to me. Next to her, the dark-haired lady, our daughter-in-law, Susan, who is the wife of Danny, our oldest son, standing next to her. Then, in the lower row, going from left to right, you first have our son-in-law, Howard [? Bellow, ?] who is holding his son, Gabriel. Standing next to his wife, our daughter, Jackie, who is holding her youngest one, Nathan. And between them is Ilana, smiling. Next to Jackie on her right-hand side is Joanna, who is Danny's and Susan's older daughter. And below Joanna, you see Julia, who is Joanna's younger sister, also the daughter of Susan and Danny. And then you see standing Andrea, the wife of Claude. Claude, Claude holds Jacob, who is his and Andrea's son. And Andrea's second son, who was born about three or four weeks ago, Joshua, is not visible except as a slight protuberation on Andrea's stomach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion for this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: The occasion for this photograph was that it was taken fairly close to Edith's and my 70th birthday and the celebration of the new Jewish year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.
